questionText: Prove that $n(n+1)(2n+1)$ is divisible by $6$ for every integer $n$.

steps.explanation: We label the expression $P$ so we can refer to it while proving divisibility by $6$.
To prove divisibility by $6$ it suffices to prove divisibility by $2$ and by $3$ separately, because $2$ and $3$ have no common factor.
The factors $n$ and $n+1$ are consecutive integers, so one of them is even, making $P$ even.
An even factor makes the whole product even, so $P$ is divisible by $2$.
We exhaust the three possible remainders of $n$ modulo $3$; the factor $2n+1$ means we must check each explicitly.
If $n$ is a multiple of $3$, the factor $n$ makes $P$ divisible by $3$.
If $n\equiv1$, then $2n+1\equiv0$, so the factor $2n+1$ is divisible by $3$.
If $n\equiv2$, then $n+1\equiv0$, so the factor $n+1$ is divisible by $3$. Every case yields a multiple of $3$.
In all three exhaustive cases one factor is divisible by $3$, so $P$ is divisible by $3$.
We have shown $P$ is divisible by both $2$ and $3$.
Because $2$ and $3$ are coprime, divisibility by each forces divisibility by their product $6$.
Hence $n(n+1)(2n+1)$ is divisible by $6$ for every integer $n$ — the fact behind the sum-of-squares formula.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $n(n+1)$ is even so $2\mid P$; checking $n\equiv0,1,2\pmod3$ shows one factor is always a multiple of $3$ so $3\mid P$. As $\gcd(2,3)=1$, $6\mid P$. $\blacksquare$
